nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef and rocky subtidal zones within the photic zone

avoidWith: heavy herbivores (tangs, rabbitfish) in small systems

imageCandidates.notes: Daniel Hershman; 800x600. In-situ underwater shot of Fauchea laciniata (type species of Fauchea) showing the whole thallus with the diagnostic reddish-purple frond and bluish-purple iridescent shimmer. Subject-dominant, in focus.

Steve Lonhart / NOAA MBNMS; 1200x800. Sharp reef photo; the iridescent violet Fauchea laciniata sits lower-left but shares the frame with a large pink Stylaster hydrocoral, so kept as a non-recommended extra.

summary: Purple Fauchea is a rare ornamental red alga (Fauchea sp., family Faucheaceae) with rubbery, branching fronds in rich reddish-purple that develop a bluish-purple iridescent shimmer under full-spectrum aquarium lighting.
careNotes:
Purple Fauchea is a specialty ornamental red macroalga in the family Faucheaceae, order Rhodymeniales (a large group within the division Rhodophyta, commonly called red algae). The genus Fauchea is found in reef and rocky subtidal habitats across the Indo-Pacific, Tropical Atlantic, and Mediterranean, typically from the surface down to about 30 m depth. Standard reef water parameters are generally advised: temperature around 24–26°C, pH 8.1–8.3, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, and specific gravity 1.023–1.026. Based on published care standards for ornamental red macroalgae in the Rhodymeniales order, Purple Fauchea tends to do best under medium lighting and has a medium growth rate, with a relatively low nutrient uptake compared to faster-growing macroalgae. Propagation in the aquarium is generally accomplished by fragmentation — healthy branch segments can be detached and reattached to rockwork to start new growth. Display placement is recommended to best showcase the alga's iridescent coloration.

MOUNT TO ROCK — DO NOT BURY IN SAND: as a red alga, Purple Fauchea will melt at the base if planted into a sand bed. Attach it to live rock using reef-safe cyanoacrylate (superglue gel), zip-tie or fishing line, or by wedging it securely into a crevice. Heavy herbivores such as tangs and rabbitfish may graze on macroalgae and are generally best avoided in smaller systems where this alga is intended as a display specimen.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is generally accomplished by fragmentation: a healthy branch segment is detached and then wedged into rockwork or secured with fishing line, zip-tie, or reef-safe superglue until it anchors on its own. Sexual reproduction — the alternating gametophyte–sporophyte lifecycle typical of red algae in the order Rhodymeniales — has not been documented in hobby aquaria.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby-trade source for this species. Product listing for 'Purple Fauchea' (Fauchea sp.). Provides identification, imagery, morphology description, and general reef suitability context. AlgaeBarn and Top Shelf Aquatics do not list Fauchea. AlgaeBase (nominal primary per source-urls.json) was inaccessible due to CAPTCHA blocking during this research session; taxonomy was confirmed via the task brief (Faucheaceae, Rhodymeniales) and cross-checked against Rhodymeniales family-level literature.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
